sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off oggi, con un VIX a 27.19 e un calo generalizzato delle borse, incluso l'S&P 500 a 6632.19 (-0.61%). Il prezzo del petrolio è in aumento, con il Brent a $103.71 (+3.24%), il che potrebbe indicare preoccupazioni per l'inflazione e la crescita economica. La curva dei tassi è ancora in compressione, con il TLT a 86.54 (-0.49%) e il rendimento del decennale USA a 4.2850% (+0.28%).
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off today, with VIX at 27.19 and a broad decline in equities, including S&P 500 at 6632.19 (-0.61%). Oil prices are rising, with Brent at $103.71 (+3.24%), which could indicate concerns about inflation and economic growth. The yield curve remains in compression, with TLT at 86.54 (-0.49%) and US 10-year yield at 4.2850% (+0.28%).

market_impact_summary.it: I mercati azionari sono in calo, con l'S&P 500 a 6632.19 (-0.61%) e lo STOXX 600 a 59.1300 (-0.55%). Il prezzo del petrolio è in aumento, con il Brent a $103.71 (+3.24%). I tassi di interesse sono in aumento, con il rendimento del decennale USA a 4.2850% (+0.28%). L'oro è in calo, a $5024.30/oz (-1.79%).
market_impact_summary.en: Equity markets are declining, with S&P 500 at 6632.19 (-0.61%) and STOXX 600 at 59.1300 (-0.55%). Oil prices are rising, with Brent at $103.71 (+3.24%). Interest rates are increasing, with US 10-year yield at 4.2850% (+0.28%). Gold is down, at $5024.30/oz (-1.79%).

audio_script_it:
Buongiorno e benvenuti nel nostro podcast di oggi. Siamo qui per discutere degli ultimi sviluppi nei mercati finanziari e nella geopolitica. 

Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off oggi, con un indice VIX a 27,19 e un calo generalizzato delle borse, incluso l'S&P 500 a 6632,19 (-0,61%). Il prezzo del petrolio è in aumento, con il Brent a $103,71 (+3,24%), il che potrebbe indicare preoccupazioni per l'inflazione e la crescita economica. 

In particolare, il mercato sta seguendo con attenzione la situazione in Medio Oriente, dove la guerra tra Israele e Iran sta avendo un impatto significativo sui prezzi del petrolio. La chiusura dello Stretto di Hormuz, uno dei principali passaggi per il trasporto del petrolio, potrebbe avere conseguenze disastrose per l'economia globale.

Dal punto di vista macroeconomico, i dati rilasciati ieri hanno mostrato un calo dell'inflazione negli Stati Uniti, con il CPI che è sceso al 2,7% rispetto al 2,8% precedente. Tuttavia, la situazione rimane preoccupante, con un tasso di disoccupazione che è salito al 4,4% rispetto al 4,3% precedente.

Le banche centrali stanno seguendo con attenzione la situazione e potrebbero essere costrette a intervenire per stabilizzare i mercati. La Federal Reserve, in particolare, sta mantenendo un atteggiamento cauto, con un tasso di interesse che rimane stabile al 3,64%.

In Europa, la situazione è altrettanto complessa, con la BCE che sta cercando di bilanciare la crescita economica con la necessità di controllare l'inflazione. I tassi di interesse sono stati mantenuti stabili, ma la banca centrale sta seguendo con attenzione la situazione.

In questo contesto, gli investitori stanno cercando di capire cosa potrebbe succedere nei prossimi mesi. La guerra in Medio Oriente è una delle principali preoccupazioni, insieme alla situazione in Ucraina e alle tensioni commerciali tra Stati Uniti e Cina.

Noi continueremo a seguire la situazione e a fornire aggiornamenti ai nostri ascoltatori. Grazie per averci ascoltato e arrivederci al prossimo podcast.

Per quanto riguarda i numeri di oggi, l'EUR/USD è a 1,1423 (-0,85%), l'S&P 500 è a 6632,19 (-0,61%), il VIX è a 27,19 (-0,37%), il TLT Bond USA 20Y è a 86,54 (-0,49%) e il rendimento del decennale USA è a 4,2850% (+0,28%).

Il prezzo dell'oro è a $5024,30/oz (-1,79%), il Bitcoin è a $71.387 (+1,27%) e il prezzo del petrolio è a $103,71 (+3,24%).

In Asia, le borse sono state negative, con il Nikkei che è sceso dell'1,16% e lo Shanghai che è sceso dello 0,82%.

Speriamo che questo podcast sia stato utile per capire meglio la situazione attuale dei mercati. Noi saremo qui domani per fornire ulteriori aggiornamenti e analisi.
audio_script_en:
Good morning and welcome to our podcast today. We are here to discuss the latest developments in financial markets and geopolitics.

The market sentiment is risk-off today, with the VIX index at 27.19 and a generalized decline in stock markets, including the S&P 500 at 6632.19 (-0.61%). The price of oil is rising, with Brent at $103.71 (+3.24%), which could indicate concerns about inflation and economic growth.

In particular, the market is closely following the situation in the Middle East, where the war between Israel and Iran is having a significant impact on oil prices. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz, one of the main passages for oil transportation, could have disastrous consequences for the global economy.

From a macroeconomic perspective, the data released yesterday showed a decline in inflation in the United States, with the CPI falling to 2.7% from 2.8% previously. However, the situation remains concerning, with an unemployment rate that has risen to 4.4% from 4.3% previously.

Central banks are closely monitoring the situation and may be forced to intervene to stabilize markets. The Federal Reserve, in particular, is maintaining a cautious attitude, with an interest rate that remains stable at 3.64%.

In Europe, the situation is equally complex, with the ECB trying to balance economic growth with the need to control inflation. Interest rates have been kept stable, but the central bank is closely monitoring the situation.

In this context, investors are trying to understand what may happen in the coming months. The war in the Middle East is one of the main concerns, along with the situation in Ukraine and trade tensions between the United States and China.

We will continue to follow the situation and provide updates to our listeners. Thank you for listening and goodbye for now.

Regarding today's numbers, EUR/USD is at 1.1423 (-0.85%), S&P 500 is at 6632.19 (-0.61%), VIX is at 27.19 (-0.37%), TLT Bond USA 20Y is at 86.54 (-0.49%) and the 10-year US yield is at 4.2850% (+0.28%).

The price of gold is at $5024.30/oz (-1.79%), Bitcoin is at $71,387 (+1.27%) and the price of oil is at $103.71 (+3.24%).

In Asia, stock markets were negative, with the Nikkei down 1.16% and the Shanghai down 0.82%.

We hope this podcast has been helpful in understanding the current market situation. We will be here tomorrow to provide further updates and analysis.

articles.title: Cold Hard Truths: Arctic Security in a Changing World
articles.snippet: This week on War & Peace, Olga speaks with Gabriella Gricius, Senior Fellow at the Arctic Institute, to discuss Arctic security, Donald Trump’s ambitions to acquire Greenland, Russia’s and China’s interests in the High North, and how NATO and European capitals are responding.

articles.title: A New Global Energy Shock
articles.snippet: When the United States and Israel launched their war of choice against Iran, the Islamic Republic pursued the most effective response at its disposal: triggering a spike in global energy prices by attacking Gulf energy infrastructure and blocking the flow of oil through the Strait of Hormuz. While the long-term effects on energy markets remain difficult to predict, rising energy prices and interest rates have provided US President Donald Trump with powerful reasons to seek an off-ramp from a war opposed by most Americans, including his MAGA base. But with Israel committed to “total victory” and a defiant Islamic Republic now mining the strait, a cascade of global economic disruption appears likely.

articles.title: Japanese Leader Wrestles with Middle East Shock in Planned Trip to Washington
articles.snippet: Crisis Group expert Matt Wheeler assesses the importance of Sanae Takaichi’s forthcoming visit to the White House, amid war, tensions with China and U.S. policy shifts

articles.title: The Iran War Could Trigger a Global Food Crisis
articles.snippet: While media coverage of Iran’s closure of the Strait of Hormuz has focused on oil prices, the implications for global food supplies are no less alarming. A prolonged closure could disrupt agriculture worldwide and place more than 100 million people at risk of a humanitarian catastrophe.

articles.title: Infrastructure Investment Is the Key to China’s Growth
articles.snippet: China’s government has now set its growth target for 2026 at 4.5-5%. With China’s GDP deflator still in negative territory and ample policy space for more expansionary fiscal and monetary policy, this is achievable, but it will require boosting both consumption and investment spending.

articles.title: Sudan’s devastating war rages on as regional rivalries deepen
articles.snippet: Yet the Iran crisis may push Gulf rivals to set aside their differences and revive stalled diplomacy on Sudan.

articles.title: The Gulf’s Tough Choices
articles.snippet: For the Gulf Cooperation Council's six member states, strategic patience may be the wisest approach to the Iran war for now. But the longer the war goes on, the more the region’s credibility as a stable global hub erodes – a vulnerability that the Islamic Republic is determined to exploit.

articles.title: Iran, War Powers, and the Power of the Purse: Leverage or Legalization
articles.snippet: Iran, War Powers, and the Power of the Purse: Leverage or Legalization

articles.title: Military Senior Service Colleges Require Reform, But There Sure Are Some Bad Ideas Out There
articles.snippet: “No one is thinking if everyone is thinking alike.” Gen. George S. Patton’s quip shows that he not only understood the advantage of audacious combat leadership, but the necessity to develop leaders and organizations that think critically and challenge assumptions. Military education is at the forefront of this endeavor in professional militaries. Indeed, last week Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth ordered a review of the military’s senior service/war colleges and the severing of ties between the department and 22 academic institutions in an effort to better prepare strategic leaders for the complexities of modern warfare.In a recent opinion article, Thomas The post Military Senior Service Colleges Require Reform, But There Sure Are Some Bad Ideas Out There appeared first on War on the Rock

articles.title: The Spheres-of-Influence Illusion
articles.snippet: For the first time since 1945, the United States, China, and Russia are converging around an authoritarian conception of sovereignty that relies on raw power rather than international law. But history suggests that dividing the world into rival blocs is more likely to produce conflict than stability.

articles.title: The Iran War’s Muddled Endgame
articles.snippet: While Donald Trump has yet to articulate clear goals for the war in Iran, he would prefer to limit the energy shock and avoid a prolonged military engagement. But Israel does not share his reservations, and Iran views the current conflict as existential, so putting the genie back in the bottle will be no easy feat.

articles.title: Bank of America: Sell Oil Above $100
articles.snippet: Bank of America is warning investors not to chase oil prices higher if crude climbs above $100 per barrel, arguing that prices at those levels tend to trigger government and central bank responses aimed at cooling the economy. The bank said oil has already been one of the biggest market movers this year. Crude prices are up nearly 70% year-to-date, far outpacing broader commodities, which are up about 41%, and gold, which has gained roughly 17%. Over the same period, the S&P 500 has fallen about 2.5%, while bitcoin has dropped around 20%. According…

articles.title: The World’s Biggest Oil Supply Shock Is Now Unfolding
articles.snippet: The International Energy Agency (IEA) has warned that the current energy crisis triggered by the war in the Middle East marks the “largest disruption” in the history of the oil market. The group made a record intervention in the market on Wednesday, with the release of 400m barrels from the strategic reserves. But the move has made little difference to the surging price of Brent crude – the international benchmark for oil – which once again eclipsed the $100 mark on Thursday on the news two Iraqi tankers had been struck…

articles.title: China’s Sinopec to Slash Refinery Rates amid Crude Supply Shock
articles.snippet: The top Asian refiner, China’s state-controlled Sinopec, plans to slash this month its refinery processing rates by 11-13% as crude supply is choked by the Middle East war, Reuters reported on Friday, quoting sources with knowledge of the refining giant’s operations. Sinopec, whose refineries account for a third of all Chinese throughput, plans to lower its crude runs by between 600,000 and 700,000 barrels per day (bpd) in March, from initial plans to process 5.2 million bpd. The loss in refinery throughput does not include regular…

articles.title: Iran’s All-or-Nothing Game in the Strait of Hormuz
articles.snippet: Brent crude is set to close above $100 for the first time since 2022 as tensions around the Strait of Hormuz intensify. ICE Brent is set to settle above $100 per barrel for the first time since August 2022, undeterred by the US’ easing of sanctions on Russia and the IEA’s mulled release of strategic petroleum stocks. The closure of the Strait of Hormuz sits front and centre to any oil price outlook, with Iran already striking at least 18 vessels since the US-Iran conflict started two weeks ago. Iran’s New Supreme Leader Goes All…

articles.title: Banks Hike Oil Price Forecasts, and Some See $150 Crude
articles.snippet: Investment banks are scrambling to revise up their oil price forecasts as the Middle East war cripples supply from the most important oil-producing region, with some expecting oil to surge to $150 per barrel or even higher if the Strait of Hormuz remains off limits for tanker traffic for several weeks. Analysts at Macquarie warned of this potential price spike, while all other major investment banks hiked their first-quarter and second-quarter forecasts. Goldman Sachs, for example, expects Brent Crude prices to average over $100…
